title: Right upper quadrant pain after a fatty meal

chart.oneLiner: 45F presents with 12 hours of constant right upper quadrant pain that began after a fatty meal, with nausea, vomiting, and fever.

opening: I've got this constant pain under my right ribs — it started after dinner and won't go away.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 45-year-old woman, uncomfortable, holding her right side. Cooperative.

HPI: Constant RUQ pain for ~12h that began after a fatty meal, radiating to the right scapula/shoulder. Nausea and several episodes of vomiting. Subjective fevers. Has had briefer episodes of similar pain after meals in the past that resolved (biliary colic) — only if asked. NO black/pale stools, NO dark urine, NO jaundice noticed (only if asked).
PMH: obesity. MEDS: none. SOCIAL: 3 children, occasional alcohol, nonsmoker.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: uncomfortable, mild distress.
- Vitals: BP 132/80, HR 98, RR 18, 38.2°C, SpO2 99% RA.
- Abdomen: RUQ tenderness with guarding; POSITIVE Murphy's sign (inspiratory arrest on RUQ palpation); no rebound elsewhere; no distension.
- No scleral icterus. Bowel sounds present. Cardiac/lungs: normal.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the prior post-meal episodes or absence of jaundice/dark urine unless asked about prior episodes, jaundice, stool/urine color.
- Murphy's sign is revealed only if the abdomen is examined appropriately.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.CBC: WBC 13.6 with left shift, Hgb 13.4, Plt 290.
labs.LFTs: AST 48, ALT 52, Alk Phos 130 (mildly elevated), total bilirubin 1.2 (near normal) — mild, NOT an obstructive/cholestatic picture.
labs.RUQ_US: RUQ ultrasound: gallstones, gallbladder wall thickening (>3 mm), pericholecystic fluid, sonographic Murphy sign; common bile duct not dilated.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Progressive PR prolongation culminating in a non-conducted P wave (dropped QRS); grouped beating.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: Progressive PR prolongation culminating in a non-conducted P wave (dropped QRS); grouped beating. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead rhythm strip showing Wenckebach Mobitz I
searchTerms: second degree AV block type 1 strip
ekg.clinicalVignette: A 68-year-old man complains of intermittent dizziness, and telemetry shows occasional dropped beats.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Retrocardiac soft-tissue opacity with an air-fluid level behind the heart (hiatal hernia).
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Retrocardiac soft-tissue opacity with an air-fluid level behind the heart (hiatal hernia). Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph showing hiatal hernia retrocardiac
searchTerms: hiatal hernia chest x-ray air fluid level
cxr.clinicalVignette: A 70-year-old woman with heartburn and regurgitation has a film showing a mass behind the heart.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential diagnosis (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Acute cholecystitis (leading,7). Biliary colic (core,3). Choledocholithiasis / ascending cholangitis (CANT-MISS,4). Acute pancreatitis (core,3). Peptic ulcer disease (core,2). Acute coronary syndrome / inferior MI (CANT-MISS to consider,3). Named leading dx w/ justification — post-fatty-meal RUQ pain, Murphy's (core,3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Acute cholecystitis. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Choledocholithiasis / ascending cholangitis; Acute coronary syndrome / inferior MI. Also reasonable: Biliary colic; Acute pancreatitis; Peptic ulcer disease.

coreActions.item: Named leading dx w/ justification — post-fatty-meal RUQ pain, Murphy's

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial diagnostic workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: RUQ ultrasound (CANT-MISS,5). CBC (3). LFTs (3). Lipase (2). BMP (1). EKG to exclude cardiac cause (bonus,1). PENALTY: shotgun panel w/ no reasoning (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG; CXR; CBC; LFTs; Lipase; BMP; RUQ US.

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = Mobitz I (Wenckebach) second-degree AV block — progressive PR prolongation culminating in a non-conducted P wave (dropped QRS) with grouped beating. CREDIT: rate & rhythm — identifies the progressive PR lengthening before a dropped beat (7); recognizes the grouped-beating Wenckebach pattern and distinguishes it from Mobitz II (5); systematic read (3). PENALTY: calling the study normal / missing the abnormality, or mislabeling it as Mobitz II (-3).
keyFindings: progressive PR prolongation before a dropped beat
steps.idealAnswer: Mobitz I (Wenckebach): progressively lengthening PR until a P wave is dropped, then the cycle repeats.

coreActions: progressive PR prolongation before a dropped beat

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = a hiatal hernia — retrocardiac soft-tissue opacity with an air-fluid level behind the heart. CREDIT: identifies the retrocardiac air-fluid level (5); recognizes it as a benign incidental hiatal hernia rather than a cavitary lesion/abscess (5). PENALTY: calling the film entirely normal / missing the finding, or mistaking the retrocardiac gas shadow for a cavity or abscess (-3).
keyFindings: identifies a hiatal hernia as benign
commonMistakes: mistaking the retrocardiac gas shadow for a cavitary lesion or abscess
steps.idealAnswer: Hiatal hernia: retrocardiac soft-tissue density with an air-fluid level — a benign incidental finding.

coreActions: identifies a hiatal hernia as benign

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Given the ultrasound and labs, what is your diagnosis and reasoning?
steps.rubric: Acute cholecystitis (6). Cites gallstones + wall thickening + pericholecystic fluid + sonographic Murphy, with a non-dilated CBD and near-normal bilirubin arguing against choledocholithiasis (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Acute cholecystitis — RUQ ultrasound shows gallstones, gallbladder wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, and a sonographic Murphy sign, with a non-dilated CBD and near-normal bilirubin arguing against choledocholithiasis.

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH biliary disease: NPO + IV fluids (CANT-MISS,4); antibiotics – Zosyn or CTX/metronidazole (CANT-MISS,5); early laparoscopic cholecystectomy during admission (6); surgery consult (4); percutaneous cholecystostomy if high surgical risk and failing abx (3); analgesia (2). PENALTY: discharging without source-control plan (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: NPO, IV fluids, analgesia. Antibiotics – piperacillin-tazobactam OR ceftriaxone + metronidazole. Early (<7 day) laparoscopic cholecystectomy during the admission; percutaneous cholecystostomy if too high-risk for surgery and failing antibiotics. Surgery consult.

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 45F presents with 12 hours of constant right upper quadrant pain that began after a fatty meal, with nausea, vomiting, and fever. The diagnosis is Acute cholecystitis, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.

reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Acute cholecystitis while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Acute cholecystitis and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
criticalInterpretationItems: Mobitz I (Wenckebach) second-degree AV block — progressive PR prolongation before a dropped beat with grouped beating.
Hiatal hernia — a benign retrocardiac air-fluid level.
imageInterpretationPearls: An incidental abnormality is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
